title: Abstract
content: Zn-Cr layered double hydroxide (Zn-Cr LDH) and noble metal (Pt, Pd, Au) loaded Zn-Cr LDHs were prepared, and the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 was evaluated.All samples exhibited photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 to CO under UV irradiation.Among them, 0.1 mass Pt-loaded LDH exhibited the highest performance.This is attributed to the fact that noble metals act as a co-catalysts, not only for the reduction of recombination of electrons and holes, but also for the enhancement of multi-electron reductive reactions.

title: Experimental
content: Zn-Cr LDH (ZnCr molar ratio142) samples were prepared by a coprecipitation method, and noble metal loaded Zn-Cr LDH samples were prepared by the photochemical deposition method.The photocatalytic reactions were performed in a top-irradiation-type quartz glass vessel at room temperature.Before the photocatalytic tests, the samples were irradiated in air using UV light for 2 days to clear the ubiquitous organic pollutants from the surface.For the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction test, 0.05 g of the sample was placed in the vessel.High purity CO 2 gas (99.995) was bubbled into ultrapure water, after which the mixed CO 2 and water vapor gases were flowed into the vessel for 15 min (Fig. S1).After flowing, the vessel was closed, and it was placed beneath 200 W Hg-Xe lamps (LA-410UV-5, Hayashi Watch-Works, Japan).The concentration of carbon monoxide (CO) was monitored by gas chromatography with nitrogen as the carrier gas (GC-2014AF, Shimadzu, 2 m Porapak-Q column ZP-17 and flame ionization detector) (see Supplementary Information for preparation of samples and characterization).

title: Results and discussion
content: The prepared Zn-Cr LDH powder tinged a pale purple.In the UV-vis spectrum of Zn-Cr LDH (Fig. S2), there were three Contents lists available at SciVerse ScienceDirect journal homepage: www.elsevier.comlocatematletabsorption peaks at 410, 570 and less than 250 nm.The trivalent Cr cations in the brucite-like layers were similar to Cr(OH) 6 3-.From comparison of the spectrum of Zn-Cr LDH and that of the reported Cr(OH) 6 3-, the 410 and 570 nm peaks can be ascribed to the d-d transitions 4 A 2g (F)-4 T 2g (F) and 4 A 2g (F)-4 T 1g (F) of a d 3 ion in an octahedral environment, respectively 16,18,19.The peak at less than 250 nm was attributed to ligand to metal charge transfer from the O 2p state to the Cr 3d or from the O 2p to the Zn 4s or 4p levels 16,20,21.The color of the Pt-, Pd-, and Au-loaded LDH powders changed from pale purple to gray, black, and purple, respectively.This observation indicates that the Pt, Pd, and Au particles were loaded on the Zn-Cr LDH particle surface.All peaks in the XRD patterns of the prepared samples can be assigned to the pattern for Zn-Cr LDH (Fig. 1).No reflections assignable to an impurity phase were observed in the XRD patterns.The intensity of the XRD patterns for Au-, Pd-, and Pt-LDHs was lower than that for Zn-Cr LDH.When Au, Pd, and Pt were deposited on Zn-Cr LDH under UV irradiation, the solution was acidified.It is considered that Zn-Cr LDH slightly dissolved in the acid solution. Fig. 2 shows that the amount of CO evolved in the presence of Zn-Cr LDH under UV irradiation increased linearly with irradiation time.Since no CO evolution was detected in the dark, it can be concluded that photocatalytic conversion CO 2 to CO proceeded under UV irradiation. Fig. 3 shows the amount of CO evolved in the presence of Pt-, Pd-, and Au-LDHs after 3 h of UV irradiation, where the black dotted line indicates the case for Zn-Cr LDH.In all samples, CO was evolved under UV irradiation, and the amount evolved after 3 h became larger than that for Zn-Cr LDH.For Pt-LDH, 0.05 and 0.1 mass Pt-loaded LDH exhibited higher CO evolution under UV irradiation, but the amount of CO evolved drastically decreased with increasing amounts of Pt loaded on the Zn-Cr LDH.In the case of Pd-LDH, 0.1 mass Pd-loaded LDH exhibited the highest CO evolution.In contrast, for Au-LDH, 0.3 and 0.7 mass Au-loaded LDHs exhibited slightly higher CO evolution than the other samples.These results indicate that the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 is enhanced by addition of the co-catalysts, and there is an optimum amount of co-catalyst that can be loaded on Zn-Cr LDH. The specific surface area (SSA) of Zn-Cr LDH, 0.1 mass Ptloaded, 0.1 mass Pd-loaded, and 0.7 mass Au-loaded LDHs, which exhibited the highest CO evolution among the Pt-, Pd-, and Au-LDHs, was 73, 61, 88, and 50 m 2 g, respectively.The photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 for these LDHs was normalized by dividing the amount of CO evolved after 3 h by the SSA.The normalized values for Zn-Cr LDH, 0.1 mass Pt-loaded, 0.1 mass Pd-loaded, and 0.7 mass Au-loaded LDHs were 0.01, 0.13, 0.05, and 0.07 molm 2 , respectively.Among them, the value of Zn-Cr LDH was the smallest.This suggests that Pt-, Pd-, and Au-LDHs exhibit higher photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 to CO compared to Zn-Cr LDH.The 0.1 mass Pt-loaded LDH exhibited the highest performance of all samples, with a value 13 times larger than that for Zn-Cr LDH. The mechanism of photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 is complex in layered double hydroxides.Thus, it is difficult to carry out a direct comparison of the performance with other photocatalysts.Here, we suggest a possible mechanism for photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 over Zn-Cr LDH.Since, when visible light (4370 nm, Xe lamp with cut filter) is irradiated onto the Zn-Cr LDH samples, CO is not detected, we considered that the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 does not occur by absorption of the 410 and 570 nm peaks attributed to the d-d transition.When UV light is irradiated onto Zn-Cr LDH, electron-hole pairs are generated.CO 2 is reduced to CO by two photogenerated electrons.In this reaction process, a reduction in electron-hole recombination is necessary for enhancement of the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 .Co-catalysts such as Pt, Pd, and Au act not only to reduce electron-hole recombination but also to enhance multi-electron reductive reactions.Since CO is formed by a two-electron reduction reaction of CO 2 , the Pt-, Pd-, and Au-LDHs exhibit higher photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 to CO due to the enhancement of the twoelectron reductive reaction.Pt-loaded LDH exhibited higher performance than Pd-and Au-loaded LDH.Since the reaction occurs on the surface of the co-catalysts, size of the co-catalysts is an important factor.Since the size of Pt is smaller than that of Pd or Au, and the number of reactive sites on Pt-loaded LDH is larger than that for Pd or Au.In contrast, although photogenerated holes are consumed by oxidizing water molecules, O 2 is not evolved significantly.Further investigation is required to clarify the cause of this phenomenon.

title: Conclusions
content: In conclusion, the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 to CO over Zn-Cr LDH and Pt, Pd, and Au-loaded LDHs was investigated.Zn-Cr LDH exhibited the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 under UV irradiation.By loading Pt, Pd, and Au onto Zn-Cr LDH, the performance was enhanced.This result indicates that the noble metal loaded Zn-Cr LDHs are useful for enhancing the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 . Fig. 1.XRD patterns of Zn-Cr LDH and Pt, Pd, and Au-LDHs. Fig. 2. Time course of CO evolution in the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 over Zn-Cr LDH. The authors are grateful to Prof. T. Akatsu (Tokyo Institute of Technology, Japan) for the XRD, to Ms. H. Takei (Kanagawa Academy of Science and Technology, Japan) and Mr. R. Motoyoshi (Tokyo Institute of Technology, Japan) for helpful support in using the UV-vis instrument.This work was supported in part by the Nippon Sheet Glass Foundation for Materials Science and Engineering, and by the project "Development Base of Advanced Materials Development and Integration of Novel Structured Metallic and Inorganic" of the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology (MEXT), Japan. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http:dx.doi.org10.1016j.matlet.2013.05.132.
